one_line_takeaway: By formalizing unlearning as a differentiable one-step gradient update and using surrogate pseudo-labels, the authors show that a small budget of injected nodes (5% of the graph, 5 edges each) can be optimized so that a legally-mandated deletion request collapses GCN test accuracy by 15-60 percentage points across Cora/Citeseer/Pubmed/Flickr and three unlearning algorithms (GIF, CEU, GA), while all pre-existing poisoning/backdoor/heuristic baselines produce far smaller or even negative drops.
key_finding: OptimAttack achieves the largest DeltaAcc of every method tested, across all 4 datasets and all 3 unlearning algorithms, while heuristic baselines (Random, Copy, NewCopy, TestCopy, TestLink) and unlearning-agnostic node-injection attacks (SBA, UGBA, TDGIA) generally produce negligible or even negative accuracy drops. From the paper's own Table 1: under GIF unlearning, OptimAttack drives Pubmed accuracy from 0.8351 (original) down to 0.2443 (unlearned), a DeltaAcc of 0.5908; Citeseer drops from 0.7381 to 0.2066 (DeltaAcc 0.5315); Cora from 0.8162 to 0.6103 (DeltaAcc 0.2059); Flickr from 0.4320 to 0.2860 (DeltaAcc 0.1460). Under CEU unlearning, comparable collapses occur: Pubmed 0.8102 to 0.2146 (DeltaAcc 0.5956), Citeseer 0.7267 to 0.2276 (DeltaAcc 0.4991), Cora 0.7904 to 0.5801 (DeltaAcc 0.2103), Flickr 0.4322 to 0.2858 (DeltaAcc 0.1464). Under Gradient Ascent (GA) unlearning: Cora 0.8258 to 0.4790 (DeltaAcc 0.3469), Citeseer 0.7345 to 0.4138 (DeltaAcc 0.3207), Pubmed 0.8279 to 0.6798 (DeltaAcc 0.1481), Flickr 0.4318 to 0.3012 (DeltaAcc 0.1306). By contrast the strongest non-OptimAttack baseline under GIF/Pubmed is TDGIA with DeltaAcc 0.2390, still far below OptimAttack's 0.5908 on the same cell; the NoAttack control shows only natural unlearning-induced drift (e.g. DeltaAcc 0.0044-0.0612 depending on dataset/algorithm), confirming the observed collapse is attack-induced rather than an artifact of the unlearning procedure itself. An ablation removing the stealthiness regularization term (lambda=0, 'w/o Reg.') shows that without it, unlearning benign (non-injected) nodes causes large collateral damage -- the paper reports Citeseer Benign F1 dropping to 0.6517 versus an original accuracy of 0.7380 -- whereas the full method with the regularizer keeps Benign F1 at 0.7286, close to the original, at negligible cost to attack strength (G1). A budget-sensitivity sweep (unlearning ratios 1-9%, edge budgets 1-20) shows DeltaAcc increases with budget but plateaus quickly, with the paper's default modest setting (5% node ratio, 5 edges/node) already achieving near-maximal degradation.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'unlearning corruption attacks' on graph neural networks (GNNs): an adversary injects a small set of labeled nodes/edges into a training graph, waits for the model owner to train a GCN on the poisoned graph, and then submits a (legally mandated) deletion/unlearning request for exactly those injected nodes. The attack is engineered so the model looks normal both before injection-driven training and immediately after node injection, but accuracy collapses on the unlabeled/test population only once the model owner actually processes the unlearning request. The authors formalize this as a bi-level optimization problem over three simultaneous adversarial goals: (G1) large post-unlearning damage, (G2) near-normal pre-unlearning utility, and (G3) no collateral damage when benign (non-attacker) nodes are unlearned. To make the bi-level problem tractable they (1) approximate the black-box unlearning operator with a one-step gradient-ascent/gradient-descent update (differentiable, enabling gradient-of-gradient optimization of injected features/edges) and (2) address the attacker's lack of ground-truth labels on the target population by training a surrogate GNN and using its predictions as pseudo-labels to build the attack loss. Edges are optimized via a sigmoid relaxation over a continuous adjacency and projected to the discrete top-B budget only at the end (to avoid the vanishing-gradient problem of iterative discrete PGD projection), and a k-hop neighborhood sparsification is used for scalability. The resulting method, OptimAttack, is evaluated against six intuitive heuristic baselines (Random, Copy, NewCopy, TestCopy, TestLink, and a NoAttack control) and three existing GNN node-injection attacks not designed for this setting (SBA, UGBA, TDGIA), across three unlearning algorithms (GIF, CEU, Gradient Ascent) and four datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, Flickr).
threat_model: Attacker capability: has access to the original training graph G (justified as realistic for social/DeFi networks exposed via public APIs/crawlers) and can inject a small budget of new nodes with attacker-chosen features, edges, and labels (framed as analogous to creating fake accounts with self-declared profile attributes). The attacker is black-box and target-model-free: no access to the victim model's architecture, injected-model parameters theta_inj, unlearned parameters theta_un, or model outputs; the attacker only knows the target is a GNN subject to some graph-unlearning process, and must approximate victim behavior via a locally trained surrogate model with similar (but not identical) architecture/training/unlearning details. Attacker goal: after the model owner (i) trains on the poisoned graph and (ii) processes the attacker's own deletion request for the injected nodes -- a request the paper argues is unavoidable to honor because it is legally mandated under GDPR/CCPA/PIPEDA 'right to be forgotten' rules -- the resulting unlearned model's accuracy on the unlabeled node population collapses, while performance was normal both during training and immediately post-injection, and unlearning of unrelated benign nodes does not trigger comparable damage. This is explicitly contrasted with (and distinguished from) both poisoning attacks (no unlearning step in the objective; reduces to poisoning only if the unlearning operator is the identity) and backdoor attacks (no inference-time trigger pattern is required; the trigger is the unlearning operation itself, which cannot be filtered by inspecting inputs).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation (Conclusion): scalability to billion-scale graphs was explicitly not the primary objective; the k-hop neighborhood restriction is described by the authors as an 'intuitive' approximation, and they state future work will refine it for larger-scale settings. Beyond this self-declared limitation, several constraints are apparent from the methodology as described in the paper: the attack assumes injected nodes are always included as labeled nodes in the victim's training set (i.e., the model owner trains on attacker-supplied labels without label verification) -- a strong assumption the paper defends by analogy to self-declared social-network profile attributes but does not empirically stress-test against label-sanitization defenses. The one-step gradient approximation to the (potentially multi-step or genuinely black-box) unlearning operator is validated only empirically via attack success on GIF/CEU/GA, not with a formal approximation-error bound. Evaluation is limited to a single victim architecture family (2-layer GCN) with cross-architecture transferability deferred to an appendix figure not captured in the extracted pages, and all datasets are standard small/medium academic citation-network or Flickr benchmarks rather than production-scale graphs. Comparison baselines explicitly acknowledge non-comparability: the paper states existing poisoning/backdoor/unlearning-attack literature is 'not directly comparable' to its setting, so the strongest evidence of superiority is relative to heuristics and repurposed injection attacks rather than a like-for-like unlearning-attack baseline (since, per the paper, none yet exists in the literature it reviewed).

quantitative_results: Strongest non-OptimAttack baseline (GIF/Pubmed): TDGIA DeltaAcc 0.2390 vs OptimAttack 0.5908
NoAttack control DeltaAcc range 0.0007-0.0612 across dataset/algorithm combinations, confirming baseline unlearning drift is small
Ablation (w/o stealthiness regularizer, lambda=0), Citeseer: Benign F1 falls to 0.6517 vs original accuracy 0.7380; full method (w/ Reg.) keeps Benign F1 at 0.7286
